verses.commentary: Led (anago, ἀνήχθη) by the Spirit into the wilderness — not a digression but the Spirit's deliberate route. Forty days recapitulates Israel's forty years (Deuteronomy 8:2). Jesus is the true Israel undergoing the same testing.
Forty days and nights of fasting echoes Moses on Sinai (Exodus 34:28) and Elijah at Horeb (1 Kings 19:8). Hunger is real — the incarnation includes physical limit. The tempter strikes at weakness.
'If you are the Son of God' — picks up the baptism declaration (3:17). Satan tests the identity God just affirmed. Bread from stones would mean using sonship for self-supply rather than mission.
Jesus cites Deuteronomy 8:3 — the very text about Israel's wilderness manna lesson. Where Israel grumbled, Jesus obeys. The word of God outranks the demands of the body.
The holy city, the temple pinnacle. Satan stages temptation at the most sacred geography. Sin enters through religious settings as readily as through carnal ones.
Satan quotes Scripture too — Psalm 91:11-12. He omits 'in all your ways' to twist protection into presumption. Scripture cited badly is more dangerous than scripture not cited.
Jesus answers from Deuteronomy 6:16 — Israel's testing of God at Massah. Faith does not stage demonstrations to extract guarantees. Testing God is unbelief in fancy dress.
Very high mountain — the geography of revelation (Sinai, Pisgah, Zion). Satan offers his counterfeit version of dominion: the kingdoms of the world without the cross.
The bargain laid bare: worship Satan, receive the kingdoms now. The shortcut around Gethsemane. All temptation reduces to misplaced worship.

Jesus cites Deuteronomy 6:13 and dismisses Satan: 'Be gone' (hypage, ὕπαγε). Three Deuteronomy quotations from Israel's wilderness chapters. Jesus succeeds where Israel failed using Israel's own scripture.
Satan departs. Angels (compare 1 Kings 19:5-7, Elijah fed by an angel) attend the victor. The fast ends in heavenly provision — confirmation of the path he chose.
John imprisoned. Jesus 'withdraws' (anachoreo, ἀνεχώρησεν) — a Matthean pattern of strategic retreat (compare 2:13-14). Ministry begins as forerunner is silenced.
Capernaum on the Sea of Galilee becomes the new base. Zebulun and Naphtali — northern tribes overrun first by Assyria (2 Kings 15:29). Geography of exile becomes geography of dawn.
Fulfillment formula again. Even the choice of headquarters is prophetically scripted.
Isaiah 9:1 quoted. 'Galilee of the Gentiles' — historically mixed population. Ministry begins in geographically and ethnically marginal territory.
Isaiah 9:2. Light dawns on darkness and the shadow of death (skia thanatou, σκιὰ θανάτου). The Messiah's appearing is described in cosmic terms — sunrise over death-shadowed land.
Verbatim John's message (3:2): 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has come near.' Continuity with the forerunner; Jesus picks up the baton seamlessly. Repentance precedes kingdom-life.
Jesus calls fishermen by the sea — echoes Elisha called from plow (1 Kings 19:19-21). Calling happens during ordinary labor, not in temple courts.
'Fishers of men' (halieis anthropon, ἁλιεῖς ἀνθρώπων) — picks up Jeremiah 16:16 and Ezekiel 47:9-10 imagery. Their old skill is reoriented to new mission. Discipleship as occupation-transformation.
Immediately (eutheos, εὐθέως) — Matthean haste. Nets abandoned without negotiation. Cost of discipleship paid in present-tense decision.
James and John with Zebedee their father, mending nets. Family business intersects with kingdom call.
Immediately again. They leave boat AND father. Discipleship requires loosening primary loyalties (compare 10:37). The cost expands as the call deepens.
Three verbs summarize Jesus' ministry: teaching, proclaiming, healing. Word and deed paired. Synagogues are the venue — ministry begins inside Israel's structures.
News spreads to Syria. Multiple categories of suffering listed — disease, demon-possession, seizures, paralysis. Jesus addresses every layer: physical, psychological, spiritual.

Crowds from Galilee (north), Decapolis (east — largely Gentile), Jerusalem and Judea (south), and beyond Jordan (east). Geographic reach is panoramic. The kingdom magnet attracts from every direction.
